entities.description: A public manually keyed and proofread electronic text selecting Zupitza's edited poem, stanza headings, manuscript and comparative milestones, footnote apparatus, trailer, dateline, and signature from printed pp. 66–82. It omits the article's German introduction and editorial discussion on pp. 57–65.

bibliographic.citation: Julius Zupitza, 'Jak and his step dame nach der Handschrift Rawlinson C 86 mit den Abweichungen des Porkington-Manuskripts,' Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Litteraturen 90 (1893), 57–82.
bibliographic.container_title: Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Litteraturen
bibliographic.title: Jak and his step dame nach der Handschrift Rawlinson C 86 mit den Abweichungen des Porkington-Manuskripts

entities.description: Zupitza's German-language article and edition. Printed pp. 57–65 introduce the textual tradition and editorial method; pp. 66–82 print the R text with P variants known through Halliwell. The article explains the milestone sigla later retained in the XML and states that three missing lines in stanza LIX were supplied from P.
entities.label: Julius Zupitza, Jak and his step dame (1893)

notes: The article title is printed with I/J typography that is commonly normalized as 'Jak'; the XML's closing trailer reads 'Iak.'
The U-M encoding begins at the first stanza on p. 66 and ends with Zupitza's signature on p. 82.

rights.basis: The article was published in 1893 and its author died in 1895, placing it beyond ordinary publication- and author-term copyright limits.
rights.component: Zupitza's 1893 article, transcription, apparatus, commentary, and typographic presentation
rights.jurisdiction: United States and jurisdictions with ordinary author-term copyright limits

bibliographic.citation: J. O. Halliwell, ed., Early English Miscellanies, in Prose and Verse, Selected from an Inedited Manuscript of the Fifteenth Century (London: Printed for the Warton Club, 1855), 46–62.
bibliographic.title: Early English Miscellanies, in Prose and Verse, Selected from an Inedited Manuscript of the Fifteenth Century

entities.description: Halliwell's transcription of ten selections from the then privately held Porkington manuscript. The poem printed without a supplied verbal title beneath item number VII on pp. 46–62 transcribes the witness now cataloged as NLW Brogyntyn MS II.1, fols. 139r–150r. Halliwell's preface says the manuscript was temporarily entrusted to him for transcription.
entities.label: J. O. Halliwell, Early English Miscellanies (1855), The Friar and the Boy

physicalDescription.extent: viii, 96 printed pages; the poem occupies pp. 46–62
notes: The next numbered selection, VIII, begins later on printed p. 62.

rights.basis: The book was published in 1855 and its editor died in 1889, placing it beyond ordinary publication- and author-term copyright limits.
rights.component: Halliwell's 1855 transcription, preface, edited collection, and typographic presentation
rights.jurisdiction: United States and jurisdictions with ordinary author-term copyright limits

entities.description: The current Middle English Texts Series student edition and scholarly introduction. It is supporting scholarship, not a source from which CME00073 was encoded.
entities.label: Melissa M. Furrow, Ten Bourdes, Jack and His Stepdame

dateStatements.label: Zupitza's dating of the poem's hands
dateStatements.notes: The current catalog dates the composite and its parts separately across a wider range; this statement is retained specifically as Zupitza's assessment of the poem's two hands.

entities.description: The direct copy-text witness for Zupitza's edition. The Tale of Jack and His Stepdame occupies fols. 52r–59r in Part II of a composite miscellany. Fol. 52 is a replacement leaf; modern scholarship distinguishes the replacement hand from the principal scribe rather than treating the whole item as a single homogeneous copying event.

holding.notes: Held by the Bodleian Library, University of Oxford.

entities.label: Bodleian Library, MS. Rawl. C. 86

physicalDescription.extent: ii later-paper leaves + 189 + i later-paper leaf, with one inserted unfoliated later-paper sheet; work at ff. 52r–59r
notes: The complete Digital Bodleian sequence includes boards, pastedowns, endleaves, the inserted flyleaf, all foliated leaves, binding edges, spine, head, and tail.

The current aggregate catalog dates the composite from the middle of the fifteenth century through the first half of the sixteenth; that range must not be collapsed into a precise date for this poem's copying.
physicalDescription.support: paper in Part II; the composite codex as a whole contains mixed materials

rights.basis: The work and witness are medieval, many centuries beyond ordinary copyright terms. This does not determine rights in modern photography, descriptions, editions, or encodings.
rights.component: medieval Middle English textual content of The Tale of Jack and His Stepdame
rights.jurisdiction: United States and jurisdictions with ordinary author-term copyright limits

entities.description: The comparative P witness, a Middle English miscellany written by sixteen scribes. The Friar and the Boy occupies ff. 139r–150r. The current official catalog calls the original restricted and directs readers to surrogates, while the Library also publishes a complete 425-canvas IIIF sequence.

holding.notes: Deposited in 1938 and purchased for the nation in 1993.

entities.label: National Library of Wales, Brogyntyn MS II.1

physicalDescription.dimensions: 140 × 105 mm; written space varies by quire
physicalDescription.format: Composite manuscript miscellany in a nineteenth-century half-calf binding
notes: The current catalog identifies sixteen scribes and dates the miscellany circa 1470.
The work ends on f. 150r, where the following item also begins.

next_steps: Treat the METS witness and early-print survey as a starting point for a separate full work-transmission graph.
Add only relations supported by direct edition or witness evidence, keeping the CME derivation path visually distinct.
openQuestions.question: How should the wider manuscript, early-print, and later chapbook tradition be modeled without conflating distinct endings and revisions with the immediate R-plus-mediated-P source of CME00073?

next_steps: Consult the full part-level Bodleian catalog data and the modern paleographical studies cited there.
Keep Zupitza's late-fifteenth-century assessment explicitly attributed until a stronger item-level statement is verified.
openQuestions.question: What is the best current date assignment for the two hands copying the poem in Rawlinson C. 86, including replacement fol. 52?

reviewNotes: The immediate lineage is not Zupitza directly to both manuscripts: R was inspected directly, while P was available to Zupitza only through Halliwell's printed transcription.

The XML contains 73 top-level edited stanzas and 438 top-level verse lines. Its apparent totals of 74 LG and 450 L elements include a twelve-line P-only alternative ending nested inside the note to the final R stanza.

This manifest traces the sources materially represented in CME00073. The poem has a much broader manuscript and early-print tradition that is described by modern scholarship but is not silently promoted into the immediate derivation graph.
The U-M legacy inventory dates the edition to 1895, but the journal title page, article, U-M item record, and modern bibliography establish volume 90 as 1893.

lineage.summary: CME00073 is the repository XML for a University of Michigan manually keyed and proofread electronic text derived from the edited-text portion, printed pp. 66–82, of Julius Zupitza's 1893 article 'Jak and his step dame.' Zupitza transcribed Oxford, Bodleian Libraries, MS. Rawl. C. 86, fols. 52r–59r (R), and recorded variants from the former Porkington manuscript (P), now National Library of Wales, Brogyntyn MS II.1, fols. 139r–150r. The P evidence is mediated: Zupitza explicitly says he knew P only through J. O. Halliwell's 1855 transcription on pp. 46–62 of Early English Miscellanies. Complete public provider PDFs of the two printed source volumes and complete provider-published IIIF sequences for both manuscript witnesses are cached locally; each manuscript bundle retains the whole published sequence and identifies the work's folio and canvas range inside it.

catalogSummary.title: Jack and His Stepdame

catalogingSubject.label: Jack and His Stepdame
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous fifteenth-century Middle English comic verse tale represented by CME00073, known across manuscript and print versions as Jack and His Stepdame and The Friar and the Boy. The cataloging subject is the medieval work rather than one immutable authorial text: surviving versions change titles and endings. This record distinguishes the work from the Rawlinson and Brogyntyn physical witnesses, Halliwell's 1855 transcription, Zupitza's 1893 edition and apparatus, the University of Michigan encoding, and the checked-in repository XML artifact.

contentParts.label: Jack and His Stepdame

locators.scheme: comparative version folios and IIIF canvases

notes: The Brogyntyn and Halliwell locators identify the P version used for comparison, not a claim that P and R are textually identical.
The twelve lines nested in the final-stanza note are an alternate P ending and are excluded from the direct R-stream metrics.
contentParts.summary: The complete R-based comic verse tale represented by CME00073, from the prayerful incipit through Jack's magical gifts, the humiliation of his stepmother and the friar, family reconciliation, and the final prayer.

assertion.notes: The estimate is based on the manuscript history; no authorial or authoritative original survives.

This is the earliest securely dated extant witness verified in this research, not a claim that the poem was composed in 1470; the catalog date is approximate even though the normalized field records its central year.

dateStatements.display: Attested circa 1470 in National Library of Wales, Brogyntyn MS II.1

extent.description: One verse tale represented by 73 top-level LG groups and 438 top-level L elements: numbered stanzas 1–71 plus 52a and 59a. The raw XML totals of 74 LG and 450 L elements include a twelve-line P-only alternate stanza nested within the note attached to the final R stanza. The encoding also retains 17 printed-page breaks (pp. 66–82), fourteen untyped R folio milestones (52r–59r), 17 Halliwell-page milestones, 79 Hazlitt-line milestones, and 340 notes. These are edition and encoding metrics, not a normalized count for every historical version.

formStatements.description: A stanzaic comic narrative; early versions use six-line, loosely accentual aa4b3cc4b3 stanzas.

genres.label: Bourde
notes: Cataloged in the Middle English Texts Series collection Ten Bourdes and within its Tales of White Magic group.

genres.label: Folk tale
notes: The boy's reward with a magic object belongs to ATU 592, The Dance Among Thorns.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: German prose in Zupitza's notes belongs to the source-edition apparatus, not to the medieval intellectual work cataloged here.
The source edition's German apparatus is described in lineage but excluded from the abstract work's language and form summaries.
The tale includes misogynistic stepmother characterization, child mistreatment, scatological humor, and comic violence; subject labels describe historical content rather than endorse it.

A complete transmission history would include additional manuscript and print versions, including a fire-damaged Cotton witness and multiple early chapbooks. This record remains partial because those non-immediate branches have not been independently manifested.

next_steps: Review the full textual and linguistic argument across the earliest R, P, Q, and E versions.
Keep the circa-1470 Brogyntyn date separately labeled as first known attestation.
openQuestions.question: Can the poem's composition be dated more narrowly than the fifteenth century without mistaking witness dates for authorial dates?

next_steps: Compare the METS dialect argument with LALME and textual work on scribal revision.
Continue to distinguish poet's dialect, scribal dialect, manuscript production, ownership, and circulation.
openQuestions.question: Does the probable Norfolk poet's dialect justify a more specific composition-place claim?

next_steps: Retain anonymous authorship unless direct scholarly evidence establishes a supported attribution.
Do not infer authorship from later owners, scribes, or printers.
openQuestions.question: Is there any defensible author attribution beyond Anonymous?

assertion.notes: The conclusion combines restricted rhyme and verbal forms; scribal revision has obscured much authorial dialect evidence.

notes: This responsibility applies to the mediated P material in the source edition, not to authorship of the medieval work.

scope.description: P variants, comparative readings, and three supplied lines mediated through Halliwell's 1855 transcription

subjects.label: Magic gifts and compulsions
notes: Jack receives an unerring bow, a pipe that compels hearers to dance, and a curse that makes his stepmother break wind when she looks angrily at him.

subjects.label: Stepmother, child mistreatment, and family conflict
Clergy, religious orders, and friars
notes: The friar is a target of anticlerical comedy in the CME version.

subjects.label: Compelled dancing
notes: The METS reference section identifies the relevant international tale type as ATU 592, The Dance Among Thorns. The URI is omitted at the identifier level because no authoritative public ATU record URL was verified.

summaries.value: Jack, underfed and mistreated by his stepmother, shares his poor meal with a hungry old man and receives three marvelous gifts: an unfailing bow, a pipe that forces everyone who hears it to dance, and a curse that makes his stepmother break wind whenever she glares at him. Jack's use of the gifts humiliates both his stepmother and a friar and eventually compels a family accommodation. Surviving versions differ especially in their endings; later printed forms add an ecclesiastical-court episode absent from the R-based CME text.

The CME representation follows Zupitza's R-based ending while retaining a P-only alternate final stanza inside the apparatus. Its 73 top-level stanza groups therefore describe the edited R stream, not a universal stanza count for every version of the work.

notes: Modern preferred title used by the Middle English Texts Series and normalized from the Rawlinson title tradition.
Zupitza reports that this wording was added at the opening of the Rawlinson copy by a later hand; spelling and capitalization are normalized for discovery.
titles.value: The Tale of Jacke and His Step Dame

notes: Title of Zupitza's source edition, with I/J normalized as Jak. The XML trailer prints 'Iak and his step dame.'

Established title for the early-print and later reception tradition; Halliwell names the item this way in his contents and discussion even though the text itself begins beneath Roman numeral VII without a verbal heading.

titles.value: God, that died for vs all

notes: Final verse line of the R-based edited text, followed by Amen and the editorially reproduced closing trailer.
